topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled envelope lay on the coffee table, a stark white rectangle mocking me. My hands, usually steady, trembled slightly as I picked it up. Inside, the news was delivered with the blunt finality of a closing door: Liam was moving to Seattle. Seattle. A whole ocean away. I stared at the city name, the letters blurring into meaningless shapes. I felt a sudden, sharp tightening in my chest, like someone had squeezed a vice around my ribs. I pushed the paper away, the sound loud in the quiet apartment. When Liam finally called, his voice was bright, full of plans and excitement for new adventures. I managed a smile that felt brittle, a performance I desperately hoped looked convincing. "That sounds amazing, L," I said, my tone carefully neutral, though the words felt like sandpaper in my mouth. I just wanted him to know that this seismic shift in his life was causing a genuine ripple of unease in mine.

[story 2]

I watched Liam pack his ridiculous collection of vintage vinyl, the boxes stacked high in the hallway, each one a physical manifestation of departure. When he finally turned to me, his eyes were shining with an almost manic energy. "It's happening, Maya! New city, new start!" He clapped me on the shoulder, a gesture that felt too forceful, too celebratory for the quiet dread pooling in my stomach. I forced my gaze to meet his, trying to keep my expression placid. A muscle twitched near my jaw. "Seattle?" I managed, the single word clipped and dry. I didn't elaborate. I didn't need to. The way he’d phrased it—as if this was some grand, inevitable destiny rather than a sudden severing—made my throat feel tight. I took a long, slow breath, trying to smooth the sudden roughness under my skin, but the tension remained coiled, ready to snap.

[story 3]

The revelation hit me during a casual evening, over takeout pizza and half-finished board games. Liam mentioned it while describing the logistics of renting a new place, his voice carrying that infuriatingly breezy tone of someone who has already mentally checked out of their current reality. "So, I’m actually going west next month," he announced, taking a large bite of pepperoni. I froze, the slice halfway to my mouth. The warmth of the food suddenly felt alien. My knuckles whitened where I gripped the edge of the table. I kept my expression carefully blank, focusing instead on the pattern of the wood grain. Internally, a furious, hot wave washed over me—a complete refusal to accept this narrative. When I finally spoke, my voice was unnervingly level, almost too calm. "That's... quite a change of scenery, Liam," I observed, letting a deliberate pause hang between the words, letting the weight of my quiet observation settle in the air between us.
